title: The Macro Minute: Can the US win its trade war with China?

Happy Thursday out there team 42, it's
your skipper here, Darius Dale, with our
macro minute for Thursday, October 23rd,
2025. As always, we'll start with the
executive summary from today's lead-off
morning note. So, let's hop right into
it. Today's key macro question is, can
the US win its trade war with China? The
short answer is no. In our view, the
Trump administration is now ratcheting
up pressure on Russia to distract the
public from the likelihood that the US
will be negotiating with China from a
position of weakness starting next week.
The analytical nuance is global energy
markets are rallying sharply today as
the US imposed sanctions on Russia's top
producers, Rosneft and Lukoil,
escalating pressure on President
Vladimir Putin to end the war in
Ukraine.
The decision drove Brent crude oil above
$65 a barrel amid fears of disrupted
global supply from one of the world's
leading producers. Brent's 5% advance is
its sharpest increase since the start of
the Israel-Iran conflict on June 13th.
Its price is rising on concerns that
India, a major buyer of Russian
products, may halt Russian imports to
add demand and add demand to the
non-sanctioned global energy market.
India's refinery executive said the new
rules make it all but impossible to
maintain Russian imports.
Elsewhere, China announced that Vice
Premier Liu He
He Lifeng will meet US officials in
Malaysia from October 24th to 27th to
resume trade talks ahead of an expected
meeting between President Xi Jinping and
President Trump at the Asia-Pacific
Economic Cooperation Summit in South
Korea.
A fragile US-China trade truce expiring
on November 10th is at risk of
dissolving if one or both sides tries to
negotiate from a position of strength.
Recall that developments from earlier in
the month threatened and ultimately
receded from the path of mutually
assured destruction.
Various members of the Trump
administration, including POTUS himself,
were quick to de-escalate.
From our April 14th lead-off morning
note, is President Trump losing his own
trade war? According to the White House,
the exemption from the 145% China tariff
and 10% global flat rate on consumer
electronics is temporary and part of a
longer-term plan. Although the move
signals Trump's willingness to
compromise and represents incremental
confirmation of our view that the
bombarded active bond market activated
the Trump put last week.
China's consistently firm tone indicates
that it believes it has the upper hand
in negotiations. At a minimum, President
Xi's historic consolidation of power
means he will likely outlast President
Trump's second term. At a maximum, China
understands and can weaponize the fact
that the other side of a trade war is a
capital war that the bloated US public
sector can ill afford lose.
As always, members of our global
investor community can trust that our
institutional-grade risk management
overlays, KISS and Dr. Mo, will help our
portfolios navigate these emergent risks
better than investors attempting to
manage risk without systematic rules or
relying on fundamental research alone.
Transitioning to our 42 macro dashboard,
as always, we'll wrap up with a question
from our community. This one's titled US
Treasury bonds. Can someone point me in
the direction of why DD describes US
Treasury bonds as the most mispriced
asset? So, um you can pick up any one of
our monthly macro scouting reports to
to to to to to review that information,
but I would say the best
the best composite of all that
information is here in our Dr. Mo or not
our Dr. Mo's view, sorry. Apologies. Our
Fourth Turning FAQ, where we show our
Fourth Turning analysis. Uh we have our
FAQ. You can search the FAQ, and then we
have our most recent Fourth Turning
webinar. You know, this this analysis
really helped us understand
a few things on terms of quantifying the
impact that the Fourth Turning has upon
the economy, policy, and ultimately
asset markets historically.
A few things. So,
on the economic side of things, what we
tend to see is elevated inflation,
elevated nominal growth.
On the geopolitical side of things, we
tend to see deglobalization, a decline
in global trade,
increase in in in in war, and and just a
general, you know, sharp elbows across
the global economy and just a lack of
cooperation. And then what we also tend
to see in Fourth Turnings is explosive
growth in sovereign debt and deficits
that is born in part by the
egregious, you know, income wealth
inequality that these Fourth Turning
periods tend to start from, which
ultimately caused the public sector
balance sheet to be,
you know, to grow at an exponential
level,
you know, into and through what is
historically ended in terms of total
into and through the total wars that
these Fourth Turning episodes have
historically concluded with. So, if
you're
a member of team 42, you can just log
into the 42 macro client dashboard and
again access the Fourth Turning FAQ here
in the
hamburger menu at the top right. And if
you're not a member of team 42, you're
in luck still because if you go to our
42 macro YouTube channel, which very
humbly, I just realized we have 27,000
folks following us on on YouTube as
well, so that's really really grateful
for that. Thank you guys all for
subscribing to that. Subscribe to it if
you don't. Appreciate the support. Just
go down if you're at the home page of
our 42 macro YouTube channel, just go to
the big wins playlist here
and then scroll down and and find the
video that says
Here's why 42 macro clients beat Wall
Street to the gold instead of bonds
thesis from October 2024. This 50-minute
video is a detailed overview of the hit
the transition we made the upgrade we
made to our KISS model portfolio last
fall, last October, to replace core US
fixed income with gold as a better proxy
from a portfolio construction
perspective, as a better tool from a
portfolio construction perspective. And
so, you know, if you think about
portfolio construction, there's really
only three ways you can play defense
in a portfolio. You can raise cash, you
not participate, raise cash, get to the
sidelines. You can diversify. So, you
know, people used to historically think
that bonds diversify stocks. Obviously,
you know, put premium diversifies, you
know, insurance, you know, that's all
diversification. You know, tail
response, all that kind of stuff, that's
all technically diversification. And
then there's trend following. You know,
so trend following raises cash but
doesn't keep you stuck there, it gets
you back invested, you know, after after
the crash. And so, we've chosen instead
of using what a lot of advisors and a
lot of investors out there are using in
terms of their
their pie chart asset allocations or
their target date funds, which are
essentially a rolling series of pie
chart asset allocations based on your
age. You know, a lot of folks are out
there have their portfolios exposed to
this concept of of playing defense with
diversification via Treasury bonds. And
in our in our opinion, based on our
Fourth Turning research, which is
obviously incredibly data-driven and and
incredibly robust,
based on that research, it's highly
unlikely that bonds are going to be a
defensive property in most investors'
portfolios over on a durable basis. And
so, as a function of that, we had to
find other alternative assets. You know,
the key takeaway I would say just the
the number one thing you should know
from our Fourth Turning presentation is
that there is a geopolitically driven
supply and demand imbalance in the
Treasury bond market. Supply going one
way, demand going another way from a
foreign demand perspective and also from
a private sector demand demand
perspective. So, we're going to see a
tremendous amount of financial
repression and monetary debasement to
make it the explosive growth in the US
public sector balance sheet more
palatable for the economy and asset
markets. And so, you're going to see
them continue to deregulate in terms of
getting the banks incrementally involved
in the in in in the market. You're going
to see them continue to legislate in
terms of stablecoins to get to
financially repress other investors in
the market. And ultimately, towards the
end of this process, which could be 5-10
years from now, towards the end of the
process, we're going to see the Fed
explicitly capitalize in the US Treasury
market when all those measures, those
piecemeal measures, start to fail. And
so, you know, the key takeaway is that
gold and Bitcoin, to to a lesser degree,
but gold specifically, is the best asset
in the world in the context of all those
dynamics. It is the asset that
institutional investors and foreign
central banks are most likely to rotate
from the Treasury bond market to.
Central banks aren't going to rotate
from the Treasury bond market to gold or
to Bitcoin. You know, the largest funds
in the world, you know, managing
trillions of dollars, you know, the
sovereign wealth funds and the and the
asset managers that manage a lot of the
sovereign wealth fund money, they're not
going to sell Treasury bonds and buy
Bitcoin. They're going to sell Treasury
bonds and buy gold. And so, we we
understood that from a from a, you know,
in terms of
constructing the KISS model portfolio,
in terms of making that upgrade to the
KISS model portfolio, which is obviously
has
thousands, growing, large and growing
number of thousands of clients around
the world, outperforming many of the
best hedge funds this year. In fact, I
just reviewed the hedge fund returns
through September and of the top 25
performing hedge funds in global Wall
Street right now, uh
KISS is outperforming all but one of
them. So,
congratulations to Bridgewater. Catch up
everyone else. We'll wrap up there.
